rationale: The citing paper uses Pham et al. (2010) in its theoretical framing as evidence that self-focused attention affects consumer outcomes in marketing, specifically by shifting attribution and satisfaction judgments. It is not just a bibliography list entry: the text explicitly summarizes that mechanism and uses it to position a gap, arguing that prior self-focus work has emphasized social/cognitive processing more than affective decision style. The cited study therefore helps justify why examining self-focus and reliance on feelings is a meaningful next step.
relation_description: The citing paper treats Pham et al. (2010) as prior empirical groundwork showing that self-focus can alter consumer evaluations via attribution processes. It builds from that foundation to investigate a different downstream process - relative reliance on feelings in decisions across multiple consumption contexts.
